primitives.name: Complex Plane of Experience
primitives.formalStatement: The Complex Plane of Experience is a two-axis classification framework for mathematical concepts: the real axis spans from pure logic (-1) to pure creativity (+1), the imaginary axis spans from pure embodied (-1) to pure abstract (+1). Every mathematical concept occupies a position on this plane.

dependencies.description: The unit circle on the complex plane is the generative center of the framework
Uses complex plane as a classification space
Domains and quadrants are defined as sets of concepts

compositionRules.yields: Functorial mapping between the Complex Plane positions and domain structures
compositionRules.example: Two concepts near each other on the plane are more likely to compose well than distant concepts, reflecting shared mathematical prerequisites

applicabilityPatterns: Classifying mathematical problems by their character and abstraction level
Organizing an entire mathematical curriculum into a navigable landscape
Determining which mathematical domains are relevant to a given problem

primitives.name: Quadrant Classification
primitives.formalStatement: The Complex Plane divides into four quadrants, each with distinct mathematical character: Q1 (Abstract+Creative): pure mathematics, category theory, topology; Q2 (Abstract+Logical): formal methods, proof theory, mathematical logic; Q3 (Embodied+Logical): applied science, physics, engineering; Q4 (Embodied+Creative): design, simulation, computational art.
primitives.computationalForm: Given a concept's plane position (real, imaginary): if real > 0 and imaginary > 0: Q1 (abstract-creative). If real < 0 and imaginary > 0: Q2 (abstract-logical). If real < 0 and imaginary < 0: Q3 (embodied-logical). If real > 0 and imaginary < 0: Q4 (embodied-creative). Border cases use nearest-domain heuristic.

dependencies.description: Quadrants are defined on the Complex Plane
Each quadrant is a set of concepts

compositionRules.yields: Multi-quadrant problem decomposition strategy
conditions: Problem has components in multiple quadrants
compositionRules.example: Engineering a bridge (Q4: creative-embodied) requires structural analysis (Q3: logical-embodied) and topology optimization (Q1: creative-abstract)

applicabilityPatterns: Quickly classifying a mathematical concept by its nature
Organizing curriculum by quadrant for balanced learning
Identifying which thinking mode a problem requires

primitives.name: Domain Positioning
primitives.formalStatement: Each of the 10 mathematical domains occupies a region on the Complex Plane defined by a center position and radius: Perception (-0.2, 0.2, r=0.4), Waves (-0.4, 0.0, r=0.4), Change (0.0, -0.2, r=0.4), Structure (-0.3, 0.5, r=0.4), Reality (0.3, -0.4, r=0.35), Foundations (-0.6, 0.6, r=0.35), Mapping (0.2, 0.4, r=0.4), Unification (0.0, 0.6, r=0.3), Emergence (0.5, 0.0, r=0.4), Synthesis (0.0, 0.0, r=0.6).
primitives.computationalForm: For a domain D with center (c_r, c_i) and radius r: a concept at position (p_r, p_i) belongs to D if sqrt((p_r - c_r)^2 + (p_i - c_i)^2) <= r. Concepts may belong to multiple overlapping domains. Synthesis, centered at the origin with the largest radius, overlaps all other domains.

dependencies.description: Domain positions are coordinates on the Complex Plane
Distance calculation uses Pythagorean distance

compositionRules.yields: Complete domain map: the 10-domain atlas of mathematical knowledge
conditions: All domain centers and radii are defined
compositionRules.example: Structure (center -0.3, 0.5) overlaps with Foundations (center -0.6, 0.6) in the abstract-logical region, reflecting how linear algebra and abstract algebra share concepts

applicabilityPatterns: Mapping which domains cover which areas of the mathematical plane
Identifying which domains overlap for cross-domain composition
Routing problems to the most relevant domain based on plane position

primitives.name: Cross-Quadrant Composition
primitives.formalStatement: The most powerful mathematical techniques combine concepts from different quadrants of the Complex Plane. Cross-quadrant composition bridges abstract and embodied, logical and creative, yielding solutions that neither quadrant alone could produce. The composition cost increases with plane distance.
primitives.computationalForm: Given two primitives at positions (r1, i1) and (r2, i2): composition distance = sqrt((r1-r2)^2 + (i1-i2)^2). Cross-quadrant distance >= sqrt(min_axis_crossing^2) > 0. Composition is valid when there exists a bridge primitive in the overlapping region.

dependencies.description: Quadrant identification determines which compositions are cross-quadrant
Cross-quadrant composition is a functorial mapping between domain categories
The plane provides the distance metric for composition cost

compositionRules.yields: Optimal cross-quadrant solution paths that minimize total composition cost
conditions: Start and end domains are identified
compositionRules.example: Solving a physics problem (Q3) using category theory (Q1): navigate through structure (Q2 border) and mapping (Q1-Q3 bridge)

applicabilityPatterns: Solving problems that require combining abstract theory with practical application
Finding creative approaches by crossing between logical and creative quadrants
Building mathematical bridges between theory and computation

primitives.name: Plane Navigation
primitives.formalStatement: Plane navigation is the technique of tracing paths through the Complex Plane from a problem's position to the primitives needed for its solution. A valid navigation path visits domains in dependency order, respecting prerequisite relationships, and minimizes total traversal cost.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Classify the problem to a plane position. 2. Identify activated domains (those within reach of the problem position). 3. For each activated domain, find relevant primitives. 4. Build a path through the dependency graph from problem to solution primitives. 5. Minimize total path cost (sum of edge weights).

dependencies.description: Navigation uses domain positions for routing
The plane is the space being navigated
Navigation paths are morphisms in the primitive category

compositionRules.yields: Problem-driven domain selection and primitive retrieval
conditions: Problem is classified to a plane position
compositionRules.example: A probability question activates Mapping domain; the navigator finds Bayes' theorem, which depends on conditional probability, which depends on probability axioms -- a clear path

applicabilityPatterns: Finding the mathematical tools needed to solve a problem
Building step-by-step solution strategies across domains
Optimizing the order in which mathematical concepts are applied

primitives.name: Position-Based Classification
primitives.formalStatement: Position-based classification maps a problem description to a Complex Plane position by analyzing its mathematical character: the logic-creativity balance (real axis) and the abstraction level (imaginary axis). Keyword patterns, domain activation signals, and structural cues determine the position.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Extract keywords from problem description. 2. Match against domain activation patterns. 3. Compute weighted average of matching domain centers. 4. Adjust for abstraction indicators (theoretical -> high imaginary, practical -> low imaginary) and approach indicators (formal -> negative real, exploratory -> positive real).

dependencies.description: Classification targets a position on the Complex Plane
Domain centers provide reference points for classification

compositionRules.yields: Bayesian problem classification that updates position with evidence
compositionRules.example: A problem mentioning 'eigenvalues' and 'vibration' initially classifies near Structure but Bayesian update with 'vibration' shifts toward Waves-Reality overlap

applicabilityPatterns: Automatically categorizing mathematical problems by their nature
Routing student questions to the right area of mathematics
Determining what kind of mathematical thinking a problem requires

primitives.name: Domain Activation
primitives.formalStatement: Domain activation determines which of the 10 mathematical domains are relevant to a given problem based on its plane position. A domain is activated if the problem position falls within the domain's region. Multi-domain activation occurs for problems near domain boundaries or in overlapping regions.
primitives.computationalForm: For problem at (p_r, p_i), compute distance to each domain center (c_r, c_i): d = sqrt((p_r - c_r)^2 + (p_i - c_i)^2). Domain is activated if d <= radius. Activation strength = max(0, 1 - d/radius). Rank activated domains by strength. Multi-domain problems have 2+ activations above threshold.

dependencies.description: Activation uses domain positions and radii
Problem position must be classified first
Distance computation uses Euclidean distance

compositionRules.yields: Complete problem-to-domain routing pipeline
compositionRules.example: A problem about 'wave interference in quantum mechanics' activates Waves (primary), Reality (secondary), and Structure (tertiary)

applicabilityPatterns: Determining which mathematical tools are most relevant to a problem
Handling multi-domain problems that span several areas of mathematics
Providing ranked domain recommendations for problem-solving

primitives.name: Multi-Domain Problem Solving
primitives.formalStatement: Multi-domain problems span multiple mathematical domains and require multi-step navigation through the dependency graph, collecting primitives from different domains and composing them. The solution involves: domain decomposition, per-domain primitive selection, cross-domain composition, and verification of each composition step.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Activate relevant domains (2+ domains). 2. Decompose problem into domain-specific sub-problems. 3. Solve each sub-problem using its domain's primitives. 4. Compose sub-solutions using cross-domain composition rules. 5. Verify each composition step for type compatibility and dependency ordering.

dependencies.description: Multi-domain problems activate multiple domains
Navigation finds paths across domain boundaries
Cross-quadrant techniques compose concepts from different quadrants

compositionRules.yields: Complete multi-domain solution with verified composition chain
compositionRules.example: Quantum computing problem: decompose into quantum mechanics (Reality), linear algebra (Structure), information theory (Mapping), and computational complexity (Emergence)

applicabilityPatterns: Solving complex problems that require knowledge from multiple mathematical areas
Integrating theoretical and applied mathematics in a single solution
Breaking down interdisciplinary problems into manageable components

primitives.name: Abstraction Gradient
primitives.formalStatement: The abstraction gradient is the technique of moving along the imaginary axis of the Complex Plane: from concrete/embodied (negative imaginary) to abstract (positive imaginary). Moving upward generalizes: specific numbers become algebraic structures. Moving downward specializes: abstract theorems become computable algorithms.
primitives.computationalForm: To generalize: identify the concrete instance, extract the essential properties, formulate as axioms/definitions (move from Domains I-III toward IV-VI). To specialize: take the abstract theorem, choose specific instances, compute concrete results (move from Domains VI toward III-I).

dependencies.description: The imaginary axis defines the abstraction gradient
Generalization is a functor from concrete to abstract categories
Abstraction is a core proof technique (prove the general case)

compositionRules.yields: Abstraction ladder: move up to find the right level of generality, then back down to compute
conditions: Problem admits both concrete and abstract formulations
compositionRules.example: To solve a specific system of linear equations: abstract to vector space (up), prove existence/uniqueness (abstract level), specialize back to matrix operations (down), compute the solution

applicabilityPatterns: Choosing the right level of abstraction for problem solving
Generalizing specific results to broader classes of problems
Making abstract mathematics concrete and computable

primitives.name: Logic-Creativity Balance
primitives.formalStatement: The logic-creativity balance is the technique of moving along the real axis of the Complex Plane: from pure logic (negative real) to pure creativity (positive real). Logical approaches use deduction, formal proof, and systematic analysis. Creative approaches use intuition, visualization, analogy, and experimental exploration.
primitives.computationalForm: For a stuck problem: if currently using logical approach (negative real), shift creative (try visualization, analogy, explore examples). If currently using creative approach (positive real), shift logical (formalize the intuition, check rigorously). Optimal problem-solving oscillates between creative exploration and logical verification.

dependencies.description: The real axis defines the logic-creativity spectrum
Logical approaches rely on formal proof techniques

compositionRules.yields: Full 2D navigation strategy: adjust both abstraction and approach simultaneously
compositionRules.example: Stuck on a proof (Q2: abstract-logical)? Try drawing a picture (shift to Q4: embodied-creative), gain intuition, then return to formalize

applicabilityPatterns: Choosing between rigorous and exploratory approaches to a problem
Breaking through mathematical blocks by shifting thinking mode
Balancing formal proof with intuitive understanding

primitives.name: Plane Distance Metric
primitives.formalStatement: The distance between two concepts on the Complex Plane determines their composition compatibility. Distance d(A,B) = sqrt((r_A - r_B)^2 + (i_A - i_B)^2) with composition cost proportional to d. Close concepts (d < 0.3) compose easily; distant concepts (d > 0.8) require bridge primitives.
primitives.computationalForm: Compute Euclidean distance between plane positions. Cost function: C(d) = 1.0 for d <= 0.3 (easy), C(d) = exp(d - 0.3) for d > 0.3 (exponentially increasing). Bridge detection: for d > 0.5, search for an intermediate primitive B with d(A,B) < 0.4 and d(B,C) < 0.4.

dependencies.description: Distance is measured on the Complex Plane
Euclidean distance uses the Pythagorean formula

compositionRules.yields: Inter-domain composition cost matrix
compositionRules.example: Distance from Perception center (-0.2, 0.2) to Foundations center (-0.6, 0.6) is 0.57, indicating moderate composition cost requiring bridge concepts

applicabilityPatterns: Estimating how difficult it is to connect two mathematical concepts
Planning the most efficient path between concepts
Identifying when bridge concepts are needed for composition

primitives.name: The Origin as Synthesis Point
primitives.formalStatement: The origin of the Complex Plane (0,0) is the synthesis point where all domains converge. Concepts at or near the origin balance logic and creativity, abstract and embodied. The Synthesis domain is centered at the origin with the largest radius (0.6), reflecting its role as the meta-domain connecting all others.

dependencies.description: The origin is the center of the Complex Plane
Synthesis domain is defined at the origin

compositionRules.yields: Origin as the natural waypoint for any cross-plane navigation
conditions: Source and target are in opposite quadrants
compositionRules.example: Navigating from Foundations (-0.6, 0.6) to Emergence (0.5, 0.0): routing through the origin via Synthesis primitives provides the bridge

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding why some mathematical concepts feel universally applicable
Recognizing the unifying role of meta-mathematical thinking
Using synthesis as a waypoint between distant mathematical domains

primitives.name: Mathematical Dependency Chain
primitives.formalStatement: Every complex mathematical concept traces back to simpler foundations through a directed acyclic graph of dependencies. A dependency chain is a path from a complex theorem back to the axioms it ultimately rests on. The length of the longest dependency chain in the MFE measures the depth of mathematical knowledge.
primitives.computationalForm: To find the dependency chain for a primitive P: recursively follow P.dependencies, collecting each target primitive. Continue until reaching axioms (primitives with no dependencies). The chain is the sequence [axiom_1, ..., axiom_k, ..., intermediate_j, ..., P]. Longest chain = graph depth.

dependencies.description: Dependency chains are the structure of mathematical proofs
Dependencies form a partial order (a set with a relation)
Natural numbers are among the deepest axiomatic foundations

compositionRules.yields: Complete decomposition of any concept into its foundational components
compositionRules.example: Central Limit Theorem -> Normal Distribution -> Variance -> Expected Value -> Random Variable -> Probability Axioms: a chain of depth 5

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding the logical foundations of any mathematical result
Finding the minimal prerequisites for learning a concept
Tracing the intellectual history of mathematical ideas

primitives.name: The Through-Line
primitives.formalStatement: The through-line is the narrative and mathematical thread connecting all 33 chapters of The Space Between, from counting to complexity. It traces a quark's journey from origin to present, passing through every mathematical layer: numbers -> geometry -> waves -> calculus -> algebra -> physics -> foundations -> mapping -> unification -> emergence -> synthesis.

dependencies.description: The through-line begins with counting (natural numbers)
Geometry emerges early in the through-line
Frequency analysis enters the through-line through waves
Calculus is a pivotal point in the through-line
Abstract algebra provides the structural foundation
Category theory reveals the deep connections
Machine learning represents the modern culmination

compositionRules.yields: Narrative-aware problem decomposition that follows the natural mathematical progression
conditions: Problem can be contextualized in the mathematical progression
compositionRules.example: Understanding machine learning (Ch 31) requires tracing back through calculus (Ch 8-10) -> linear algebra (Ch 11-14) -> probability (Ch 25) -- the through-line reveals the prerequisites

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding how mathematics builds on itself from foundations
Seeing the connections between apparently unrelated mathematical fields
Using the mathematical progression as a guide for learning

primitives.name: Foundational Decomposition
primitives.formalStatement: Foundational decomposition is the technique of reducing any problem to Domain I primitives (numbers, distance, angles) and building back up through intermediate domains. Every mathematical concept can be expressed as a composition chain starting from perception primitives, passing through required intermediate layers.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Express the problem in its natural domain. 2. Identify the highest-level primitives needed. 3. Follow dependency chains downward until reaching Domain I axioms. 4. Verify each step is valid. 5. Execute the composition chain upward to produce the solution. This is the 'Euclid principle': complex truths from simple axioms.

dependencies.description: Decomposition follows dependency chains
Navigation guides the decomposition path
Pythagorean theorem is a classic example of foundational decomposition
The unit circle is a generative center for decomposition

compositionRules.yields: Complete problem solution from first principles
conditions: All dependency chains terminate at axioms
compositionRules.example: Prove the Central Limit Theorem from axioms: start from probability axioms, build up random variables, expected value, variance, characteristic functions, and arrive at convergence to normal distribution

applicabilityPatterns: Solving problems from first principles when standard techniques fail
Understanding a concept by tracing its axiomatic roots
Building complete mathematical arguments from simple beginnings

primitives.name: Compositional Verification
primitives.formalStatement: Compositional verification checks each step of a composition chain against the properties of its constituent primitives. Every composition step must satisfy: type compatibility (output type of step n matches input type of step n+1), dependency ordering (later steps depend on earlier ones), and domain consistency (cross-domain compositions have valid bridge rules).
primitives.computationalForm: For each composition step [A compose B -> C]: 1. Check A.type is compatible with B's composition rule conditions. 2. Verify B appears in A.compositionRules (or vice versa). 3. Check that yields description matches C. 4. Verify no circular dependencies. 5. Mark step as verified.

dependencies.description: Decomposition produces chains that need verification
Verification is a form of proof checking
Composition verification checks categorical composition laws

compositionRules.yields: Verified proof chain with every step justified
conditions: Each composition step passes type check
compositionRules.example: Verifying a linear algebra proof: each step applies a theorem whose prerequisites were established in previous steps

applicabilityPatterns: Checking the correctness of mathematical arguments step by step
Ensuring that composition chains are valid and complete
Detecting errors in mathematical reasoning

primitives.name: Progressive Disclosure
primitives.formalStatement: Progressive disclosure is the pedagogical principle that mathematics reveals itself in layers of increasing abstraction: counting -> arithmetic -> algebra -> analysis -> topology. Each layer builds on the previous, and concepts should be introduced only when their prerequisites are established.
primitives.computationalForm: Order primitives by dependency depth: depth-0 are axioms, depth-n requires at least one depth-(n-1) primitive. Present primitives in non-decreasing depth order. For a given learner's current knowledge set K, the frontier of learnable concepts is: {P : P.dependencies subset K}.

dependencies.description: Disclosure order follows dependency depth
The through-line exemplifies progressive disclosure in narrative form

compositionRules.yields: Optimal learning paths that respect prerequisite ordering
compositionRules.example: To learn Fourier analysis: first master trigonometry (Ch 2), then complex exponentials (Ch 3), then series (Ch 5), then integral transforms (Ch 24)

applicabilityPatterns: Designing mathematical curricula that build knowledge systematically
Determining what a learner needs to know before tackling a new concept
Presenting complex mathematics in a learnable sequence

primitives.name: Cross-Domain Bridge
primitives.formalStatement: Cross-domain bridges are specific mathematical connections that link seemingly separate domains: Fourier transform bridges Waves and Mapping, Noether's theorem bridges Foundations and Unification, gradient descent bridges Change and Emergence, probability bridges Mapping and every applied domain.

dependencies.description: Fourier transform is a key cross-domain bridge (Waves <-> Mapping)
Noether's theorem bridges Foundations and Unification
Functors are the formal mechanism for cross-domain bridges
Calculus bridges Change to many other domains

compositionRules.yields: Bridge-mediated navigation between distant domains
conditions: Source and target domains are not directly adjacent
compositionRules.example: Connecting Perception (Ch 1-3) to Emergence (Ch 28-31): bridge through Change (calculus) and Structure (linear algebra)

applicabilityPatterns: Discovering unexpected connections between mathematical fields
Using one mathematical tool to solve problems in another domain
Recognizing when a concept from one domain illuminates another

primitives.name: Mathematical Completeness
primitives.formalStatement: Mathematical completeness in the MFE context means that the 10-domain structure covers the mathematical landscape sufficiently for problem decomposition. While no finite system captures all mathematics, the 10 domains form a basis -- any well-posed mathematical problem can be decomposed into primitives from these domains.

dependencies.description: Completeness requires all domain positions to cover the plane
Completeness is analogous to a basis spanning a vector space
Coverage analysis uses set union of domain regions

compositionRules.yields: Verification that every plane position is within reach of at least one domain
conditions: Union covers the relevant plane area
compositionRules.example: The 10 domain regions, with overlaps, cover the [-1,1]x[-1,1] plane sufficiently: every point is within 0.5 of at least one domain center

applicabilityPatterns: Verifying that the domain structure is adequate for problem-solving
Identifying potential gaps in mathematical coverage
Justifying the 10-domain organization as a practical classification system

primitives.name: Inverse Operations as Navigation
primitives.formalStatement: Every mathematical operation has an inverse; navigating backward through the dependency graph uses inverses. Differentiation/integration, encoding/decoding, abstraction/specialization, composition/decomposition are all inverse pairs. Inverse navigation enables both forward construction and backward analysis.
primitives.computationalForm: For a composition chain A -> B -> C -> D: the inverse chain is D^{-1} -> C^{-1} -> B^{-1} -> A^{-1}. Not all operations have exact inverses (information may be lost), but approximate inverses often suffice. Key pairs: derivative/integral, FFT/inverse FFT, encrypt/decrypt, generalize/specialize.

dependencies.description: Inverse navigation reverses dependency chains
Derivative/integral is the canonical inverse pair
Forward/inverse Fourier transform is an exact inverse pair

compositionRules.yields: Bidirectional problem solving: decompose forward OR reconstruct backward
conditions: Inverse operations exist for each composition step
compositionRules.example: Solving an integral equation: instead of composing forward (anti-differentiate), navigate backward from the solution form using differentiation

applicabilityPatterns: Solving problems by working backward from the desired result
Understanding mathematical operations through their inverses
Using inverse transforms to decode or reconstruct information

primitives.name: Scale Invariance in Mathematics
primitives.formalStatement: The same structural patterns -- groups, symmetries, transformations, fixed points, dualities -- appear at every level of mathematical abstraction. Group structure appears in integers (Z), matrices (GL(n)), symmetries (S_n), and gauge theories (SU(3)). This scale invariance means that understanding the pattern at any level transfers to all others.

dependencies.description: Group is the exemplary scale-invariant pattern
Functors preserve structure across levels of abstraction
Mathematical scale invariance parallels fractal self-similarity

compositionRules.yields: Structure-preserving transfer of insights between abstraction levels
conditions: Source and target share the structural pattern
compositionRules.example: Understanding group structure in permutations (finite, concrete) transfers to Lie groups (infinite, continuous) via the same axioms

applicabilityPatterns: Recognizing familiar mathematical patterns in new contexts
Transferring techniques from one domain to another based on shared structure
Understanding why mathematical structures repeat across levels of abstraction

primitives.name: Emergence from Axioms
primitives.formalStatement: Complex mathematical theorems emerge from simple axioms through structured composition -- the Euclid principle. From 5 axioms, Euclid derived all of plane geometry. From ZFC set theory axioms, all of modern mathematics can be constructed. This emergence is the central miracle of mathematics: finite rules generate infinite structure.

dependencies.description: Proofs are the mechanism of emergence from axioms
Natural numbers emerge from Peano axioms -- the simplest example
Emergence follows dependency chains from axioms to theorems

compositionRules.yields: Complete axiomatic reconstruction of any mathematical result
compositionRules.example: Euclid's Elements: from 5 postulates and 5 common notions, 465 propositions covering plane and solid geometry

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding how complex mathematics arises from simple starting points
Building mathematical systems from minimal axiom sets
Appreciating the generative power of formal systems

primitives.name: Unity of Mathematics
primitives.formalStatement: Category theory (Domain VII) reveals that apparently different mathematical structures share common patterns. Functors map between domains, preserving essential structure. The unity of mathematics means that insights in one domain often have counterparts in others, discoverable through functorial connections.

dependencies.description: Category theory provides the formal framework for mathematical unity
Functors are the formal bridge between mathematical structures
Cross-domain bridges are the concrete manifestation of mathematical unity

compositionRules.yields: Systematic discovery of analogies between mathematical domains
conditions: Functor between source and target categories exists
compositionRules.example: Stone duality: topology and Boolean algebra are connected by a contravariant functor, allowing algebraic proofs of topological theorems and vice versa

applicabilityPatterns: Discovering deep connections between different areas of mathematics
Using category theory to transfer results between fields
Understanding why the same theorems appear in different guises across mathematics

primitives.name: Mathematical Beauty as Signal
primitives.formalStatement: The aesthetic quality of a mathematical result often indicates fundamental truth -- a meta-heuristic for navigation. Euler's identity e^{i*pi} + 1 = 0, the Pythagorean theorem, and Noether's theorem are considered beautiful because they reveal deep, unexpected connections with minimal complexity. Beauty serves as a navigation signal on the Complex Plane.
primitives.computationalForm: Beauty heuristic: a result is likely fundamental if it (1) connects multiple domains with minimal notation, (2) reveals unexpected equivalences, (3) has a simple statement with deep consequences, (4) exhibits symmetry or duality. Use as a soft signal for prioritizing exploration paths on the Complex Plane.

dependencies.description: Beautiful results often reveal mathematical unity
Cross-domain results are often considered beautiful
The Pythagorean theorem is a canonical example of mathematical beauty

compositionRules.yields: Beauty-guided exploration: prioritize elegant paths through the mathematical landscape
conditions: Some paths are simpler or more symmetric
compositionRules.example: Euler's formula e^{ix} = cos(x) + i*sin(x) connects trigonometry, exponentials, and complex numbers in a single identity -- its beauty signals its fundamental importance

applicabilityPatterns: Using aesthetic intuition to guide mathematical exploration
Identifying results that are likely to be fundamental or widely applicable
Appreciating mathematical elegance as an indicator of deep truth

primitives.name: Proof as Path
primitives.formalStatement: A mathematical proof IS a path through the dependency graph from axioms to theorem. Each step cites a previously established result (a primitive in the graph) and applies a composition rule. The proof is valid if and only if the path is valid: every step uses correctly composed primitives with satisfied dependencies.
primitives.computationalForm: A proof of theorem T consists of a sequence of steps [S_1, ..., S_n] where: S_1 cites axioms, each S_k cites primitives established in S_1, ..., S_{k-1} or existing axioms/theorems, and S_n establishes T. Verify by checking that each step's dependencies are satisfied by preceding steps.

dependencies.description: A proof traces a path through the dependency graph
Proof techniques are the steps in the path
Each proof step must pass compositional verification

compositionRules.yields: Mechanically verified proof chain
conditions: All proof steps are formalized as primitive compositions
Each step passes type and dependency checks
compositionRules.example: Proving the irrationality of sqrt(2): path from rational number definition -> proof by contradiction -> Pythagorean theorem -> number theory (even/odd) -> contradiction -> QED

applicabilityPatterns: Understanding proofs as paths through a graph of mathematical knowledge
Verifying the completeness and correctness of mathematical arguments
Discovering new proofs by finding alternative paths through the dependency graph

primitives.name: Tiered Knowledge Loading
primitives.formalStatement: Mathematical knowledge operates in three tiers: summary (domain names, top primitives, activation patterns), active (full primitives for activated domains), and reference (complete dependency graphs and composition rules). Only the tier needed for the current task should be loaded, minimizing cognitive and computational overhead.
primitives.computationalForm: Summary tier (~4K tokens): domain list, top-5 primitives per domain, activation patterns. Active tier (~15K): full primitive details for 1-3 activated domains. Reference tier (~40K): complete graph with all edges and composition rules. Load summary always, active on domain activation, reference never in production (only for verification).

dependencies.description: Activation determines which tier to load
Information-theoretic approach to knowledge compression
The plane provides the organization structure for tiered loading

compositionRules.yields: Efficient on-demand knowledge retrieval
compositionRules.example: A calculus problem activates Change domain: load Change active tier (full primitives), keep other domains at summary tier

applicabilityPatterns: Managing the amount of mathematical knowledge loaded for a task
Optimizing computational resources when navigating the mathematical landscape
Progressive detail loading based on problem requirements

primitives.name: Composition Type System
primitives.formalStatement: The composition type system defines three ways primitives compose: sequential (output of A feeds into B), parallel (A and B contribute independently to a result), and nested (A operates inside the framework of B). Type compatibility is checked by verifying that the yielded result matches expected inputs at each composition step.
primitives.computationalForm: Sequential: A -> B means output type of A matches input type of B. Parallel: A || B means both contribute to a combined result. Nested: A(B) means B operates within A's structure. Type check: for each CompositionRule, verify conditions are satisfiable and yields is well-defined.

dependencies.description: Type system enables compositional verification
Composition types correspond to categorical composition patterns
Type compatibility is checked via set membership

compositionRules.yields: Type-safe mathematical composition chains
conditions: All composition steps have declared types
compositionRules.example: FFT (algorithm) -> Filter Design (technique): sequential composition is valid because FFT output (frequency spectrum) is the input type for filter specification

applicabilityPatterns: Ensuring mathematical compositions are well-typed before execution
Catching composition errors before they propagate through a proof
Designing valid composition chains by type matching

primitives.name: Graceful Degradation
primitives.formalStatement: When no valid decomposition exists for a problem (the activated domains lack sufficient primitives, or composition chains break), the system degrades gracefully: report the partial decomposition achieved, identify the gap, and suggest which domains or primitives would be needed to complete the solution.
primitives.computationalForm: 1. Attempt full decomposition. 2. If decomposition fails at step k: report steps 1..k-1 as valid. 3. Identify the missing primitive or broken composition. 4. Check which domain the gap falls in (by plane position). 5. Suggest: 'This problem requires a primitive near position (r, i) in domain D that we don't have.'

dependencies.description: Navigation reveals where decomposition breaks down
Gap identification uses domain activation analysis
Distance metric identifies how far the gap is from known primitives

compositionRules.yields: Informative failure reporting with gap analysis
compositionRules.example: Problem requires topology + probability composition, but no bridge primitive exists: report partial decomposition through topology, identify gap at (0.3, 0.4), suggest probability-topology bridge needed

applicabilityPatterns: Handling problems that exceed current mathematical coverage
Providing useful partial results when complete solutions are impossible
Identifying gaps in mathematical knowledge for future learning

primitives.name: Observation-Based Learning
primitives.formalStatement: Problem-solving episodes generate observations that refine the mathematical landscape: which primitives are used together, which compositions succeed or fail, which domain activations are accurate. These observations feed back into improved activation patterns, composition costs, and path caching.
primitives.computationalForm: For each problem-solving episode, record: (problem_hash, plane_position, domains_activated, primitives_used, composition_path, result, user_feedback). On positive feedback: strengthen activation patterns, cache successful paths, reduce composition costs for used edges. On negative: weaken patterns, increase costs.

dependencies.description: Navigation generates the episodes to learn from
Bayesian updating of activation pattern confidence
Analogous to training a neural network from experience

compositionRules.yields: Bayesian refinement of domain activation patterns from problem-solving observations
compositionRules.example: After 10 episodes where calculus problems also used linear algebra, increase the co-activation weight between Change and Structure domains

applicabilityPatterns: Improving mathematical problem-solving accuracy over time
Adapting to a user's problem-solving patterns and preferences
Building a personalized mathematical knowledge base

primitives.name: Composition Path Caching
primitives.formalStatement: Previously successful composition paths are cached by problem type (keyed by plane position and activated domains). When a similar problem is encountered, the cached path provides near-instant resolution without re-navigating the graph. Cache entries are validated periodically and evicted if the graph structure changes.
primitives.computationalForm: Cache key: (quantized_plane_position, sorted_domain_set). Cache value: (composition_path, success_count, last_used). On problem arrival: compute key, look up cache. If hit and success_count > threshold: use cached path directly. If miss: navigate graph, solve, cache result. Evict entries with success_count below threshold or older than TTL.

dependencies.description: Caching stores results of prior navigations
Problem classification provides the cache key
Observations determine which paths to cache

compositionRules.yields: Progressively faster problem resolution through cached experience
conditions: Cache has been populated by prior episodes
New problem matches a cached key
compositionRules.example: First time solving a Fourier analysis problem: full navigation takes 5 steps. Tenth time: cache hit resolves instantly with the same composition path

applicabilityPatterns: Accelerating repeated problem types to near-instant resolution
Building an experience base that improves mathematical problem-solving speed
Optimizing resource usage by avoiding redundant graph navigation
